planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost into well-drained soil in full sun; seeds germinate readily in warm soil without pre-treatment.

directions: American Basketflower is a showy native annual of prairies and roadsides across the south-central United States, producing large (3–4 in), lavender-pink, fragrant flower heads with an intricate basketwork of papery bracts beneath each bloom from June through August. Despite its common name and thistle-like appearance, it is not a true thistle and bears no sharp spines, making it safe to handle and ideal for mixed wildflower meadows and pollinator gardens. Sow seeds directly into lean, well-drained soil in full sun at or just after last frost; plants establish quickly and bloom within 60–90 days, requiring no irrigation once roots are established in typical prairie or roadside soils. The fragrant flowers are exceptional for native bees, butterflies, and goldfinches, which relish the seeds as they ripen in late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow among established native grasses at or just after last frost; seeds require contact with host grass roots for successful establishment.

directions: Purple False Foxglove is a native annual hemi-parasite of eastern North American prairies, pine savannas, and wet meadows, drawing supplemental nutrition through root connections to neighboring grasses while also performing its own photosynthesis. To grow it successfully, direct sow seeds among established native warm-season grasses such as little bluestem or broomsedge in full sun — without host grass roots present, germination and survival rates drop sharply. The plant produces a profusion of showy, rosy-purple, trumpet-shaped flowers with spotted throats from August through October, providing a critical late-season nectar source for native bees and supporting the caterpillars of the buckeye butterfly. Sow seeds on the soil surface at or just after last frost and keep the planting moist until germination; once established, plants are largely self-sufficient.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost directly into soil already occupied by native warm-season grasses; hemi-parasitic roots require close proximity to host plants for establishment.

directions: Clustered False Foxglove is a native annual hemi-parasite of the southeastern coastal plain, found in pine savannas and flatwoods where it attaches to the roots of nearby warm-season grasses to supplement its own photosynthesis; sow seeds directly among established native grasses such as wiregrass or broomsedge, as plants will fail to thrive in bare soil without host root contact. Its crowded, fascicled (clustered) leaf arrangement gives the stems a distinctively bushy, fine-textured appearance, setting it apart from other Agalinis species. From late summer through fall, it produces abundant rosy-pink trumpet flowers that attract native bees and support specialist pollinators in southeastern savanna restorations. Scatter seeds on the soil surface among host grasses at last frost and avoid transplanting, as disturbed roots disrupt the critical host-parasite connection.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost into lean, well-drained soil among established native grasses; close proximity to host grass roots is essential for successful germination and growth.

directions: Bluehearts is a striking native annual hemi-parasite of southeastern coastal plain pine savannas, attaching to the roots of nearby native grasses to obtain additional water and nutrients while still photosynthesizing on its own — for garden success, direct sow seeds into an area already colonized by native warm-season grasses such as little bluestem, wiregrass, or broomsedge, as plants cannot establish without host root contact. Slender, rough-textured stems rise from a basal rosette and bear erect spikes of vivid violet-blue tubular flowers from summer through fall, making Bluehearts one of the most visually stunning of all southeastern savanna wildflowers. It thrives in lean, sandy, or gravelly soils in full sun and does not tolerate shade, competition from dense vegetation, or artificially enriched garden soils. Once sown into a native grass planting, it often self-seeds in subsequent years, gradually becoming a reliable component of a savanna-style wildflower garden.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost into open, sandy soil near the root zones of oaks or native shrubs that will serve as hosts for this hemi-parasitic annual.

directions: Yaupon Blacksenna is a native annual hemi-parasite of the southeastern United States, growing in open, sandy longleaf pine habitats and roadsides where it parasitizes the roots of oaks and native shrubs to supplement its photosynthesis — direct sow seeds near the root zones of established native oaks or shrubs, as plants depend on host root contact and will not persist in open, host-free soil. Its finely dissected, ferny, Senna-like foliage gives plants an airy, lacy texture quite unlike most other native annuals, and small bright yellow flowers appear throughout summer and fall. It performs best in poor, well-drained, sandy soils in full sun and declines quickly in heavy clay or high-fertility garden beds. Yaupon Blacksenna is an excellent botanical specimen for longleaf pine garden restorations and is an important nectar plant for specialist native bees.
